text:
The inclusion of new Olympic sports is a long-standing tradition, but one Australian star athlete has raised concerns a staple event could be axed after Paris 2024.

Breakdancing, men’s artistic swimming and skateboarding will all be on the agenda at the 2024 Paris Olympics.

But there are fears for the future of a sport that has been part of the Games for 120 years.

Tokyo bronze medallist Harry Garside said it would be a “crime” if boxing was scrapped from the Olympic line-up after the 2024 Games.

“It’s more about the young up-and-coming people who dream of this,” he said.

“All of our dreams started somewhere and whether you’re a boxer or you’re from another sport we all love the Olympic movement, that’s why we’re here, we love wearing the green and gold, we love fighting for our country and I think to take that away from some young people around the world would be a crime.”

Last year the International Olympic Committee expelled the International Boxing Association from the Olympic movement amid concerns about refereeing, financial stability and governance.

While the boxing tournament at the Paris Games has been organised by the IOC, the sport is yet to be included in the initial program for the 2028 Los Angeles Games, raising fears it will be excluded.

Garside, who is a plumber by trade, said boxing was part of the ancient Olympiad, and it was “terrifying” to think it might be scrapped from the Olympics.

“Boxing is historically a poor man’s sport, and if you look around the world it’s the third-world countries that do the best,” he said.

“It’s the countries that come from these really rough areas and they’re the ones that come and win gold medals, and I think to take that away from people like that is a crime and I would hate to see it.

“Hopefully they can nut it out, I truly believe they will.  Boxing deserves to be in the Olympics.  It has saved many people’s lives and I have seen it save many people’s lives.”

Men’s boxing first featured in the modern Olympics in 1904, while women have been on the card since 2016.

Australia’s boxing team captain, Caitlin Parker, is one of six Aussie women competing in the sport at the Games.

She said she would be gutted if boxing was dropped from the Olympics.

“It has been just such a massive part of my life, the Olympics, I’ve always been obsessed about it, and it makes me so sad to think that kids who look up to us or have the same dream, that that’s going to be shattered for them,” she said.

“Especially (for) women, we’re just starting to create history in this sport and to go from only four women being qualified for boxing and now we’ve got six women, half the team, and we’re seeing the growth in Australia and around the world in women’s boxing.  Like Harry said, it would be an absolute crime if boxing was out of the next Olympics but … I do believe it will be there, in LA, I just hope everything goes right for it.”
